name: Comillas to Collado de Carmona Loop
name_original: Comillas, Marisma de Zapedo, San Vicente de la Barquera, Las Brañas, Collado de Carmona – ruta circular
description: This difficult 54.5-mile (87.7 km) road cycling route offers spectacular panoramas and significant elevation gain through Parque Natural de
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Comillas offer a diverse landscape for cyclists, characterized by coastal stretches, rolling hills, and significant elevation gains. The region features a mix of well-paved secondary roads and access routes, providing varied terrain. Cyclists can navigate through natural features such as Oyambre Natural Park and the Zapedo marsh, with routes often presenting views of the Cantabrian Sea. The area's topography includes both gentle slopes and steeper sections, catering to different fitness levels. Best road cycling routes around Comillas The most popular road cycling route is Comillas to Collado de Carmona Loop, a 54.5 miles (87.7 km) trail that takes 4 hours 41 minutes to complete. This difficult route features over 1,600 meters of elevation gain and offers expansive panoramas. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Oyambre Natural Park – Views of the Zapedo marsh loop from Comillas, a moderate 24.4 miles (39.3 km) path. This route provides views of the Zapedo marsh and the protected coastal area of Oyambre Natural Park. Local road cyclists also love the San Vicente de la Barquera – Views of the Zapedo marsh loop from Comillas, a 17.6 miles (28.4 km) trail leading through coastal areas and offering views of the Zapedo marsh, often completed in about 1 hour 36 minutes. Road cycling around Comillas is defined by coastal views, the Zapedo marsh, and varied terrain with significant elevation changes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to those requiring very good fitness. The routes in Comillas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 49 reviews. More than 2,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Comillas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Comillas?
A: Comillas offers a diverse selection of road cycling routes, with over 10 options catering to various fitness levels. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, averaging 4.5 stars from over 49 reviews.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or less experienced cyclists in Comillas?
A: Yes, Comillas provides several moderate road cycling routes perfect for those looking for a less strenuous ride. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31890990" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oyambre Natural Park – Views of the Zapedo marsh loop from Comillas&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 39.3 km path offering scenic views without extreme elevation.

Q: What are the most challenging road cycling routes around Comillas?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Comillas features several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005115400/comillas-to-collado-de-carmona-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Comillas to Collado de Carmona Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 87.7 km trail with over 1,600 meters of elevation gain, providing expansive panoramas through Parque Natural de Oyambre.

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect on road cycling routes in Comillas?
A: Road cycling routes around Comillas offer a stunning mix of coastal views, rolling hills, and natural parks. You'll often find yourself riding alongside the Cantabrian Sea, through the protected landscapes of Oyambre Natural Park, and past the unique Zapedo marsh.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or attractions along the road cycling routes in Comillas?
A: Many routes offer views or pass near significant attractions. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/344751" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic center of Comillas&lt;/a&gt;, admire the modernist architecture of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/333807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Capricho de Gaudí&lt;/a&gt;, or cycle past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/333494" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Vicente de la Barquera&lt;/a&gt;. Natural highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5406017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oyambre Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1507066" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Rabia Estuary&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Comillas?
A: The best time for road cycling in Comillas is generally during the spring and autumn months when the weather is milder and more stable. Summers can be pleasant, but it's advisable to check local forecasts for optimal conditions, as the region can experience varied weather.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available in Comillas?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in Comillas are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31879491" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Vicente de la Barquera – Views of the Zapedo marsh loop from Comillas&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38285816" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palacio de Sobrellano – Izarra Pilgrims' Hostel loop from Comillas&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can cyclists find amenities like cafes or restaurants in Comillas?
A: Comillas, being a charming town, offers numerous cafes, restaurants, and local establishments where cyclists can refuel. The historic center is particularly rich in options, providing a perfect stop for a break or a meal after your ride.

Q: Is bike rental or repair available in Comillas?
A: While specific komoot data on bike rental and repair shops isn't available, as a popular tourist destination, Comillas and nearby larger towns are likely to have facilities for bike rental and minor repairs. It's recommended to check local listings or inquire upon arrival.

Q: Are road cycling routes in Comillas suitable for families?
A: While most road cycling routes in Comillas involve significant elevation changes and shared roads, some of the moderate, shorter loops might be suitable for families with older, experienced children. Always assess the route's difficulty, traffic, and distance to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: What are the parking options for cyclists near Comillas trailheads?
A: Comillas offers various parking options within the town, including public car parks. When planning your ride, consider parking in central areas or designated spots that provide easy access to the start of your chosen route. Always check local signage for parking regulations.

Q: Can I bring my dog on road cycling routes in Comillas?
A: Road cycling routes are generally not suitable for dogs due to safety concerns with traffic, speed, and the nature of the terrain. For activities with your dog, consider exploring walking or hiking trails in the region that are specifically designated as dog-friendly.
tips: Spectacular views from the road towards the Zapedo marsh. One of the wetlands that form at the mouths of the estuaries in the Cantabrian Sea and are home to various marine species and aquatic birds. Many of them play a fundamental role in the town's economy.

This is a classic stretch of road with good asphalt from which you can see the sea to the north and the impressive peaks of Europe to the south. From this confluence of sea and high mountains it is called the Cantabrian coast. The mountains form a cornice that is influenced by the sea air.

With 500 meters in length, the bridge connects the town across the length and breadth of the San Vicente estuary. The first bridge was made of wood; then a stone one was built in the 15th century. On this, the current one was finished, during the reign of Carlos III. It represents the most traditional access road to reach San Vicente de la Barquera. When it was built in medieval times, it was listed as one of the most impressive works of naval engineering. There are privileged views of the estuary.
Great paths for biking and the views are spectacular! Almost free of traffic and in perfect condition. Watch out for cows and cow pats on the road!

faqs.question: Is this circular route suitable for beginners or less experienced cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this is a challenging route classified as 'difficult' for race bikes. It covers 87.7 kilometers with a significant elevation gain of 1,680 meters, requiring a very good level of fitness. It's considered a 'Hard road ride'.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular route?
faqs.answer: The route is estimated to take approximately 4 hours and 41 minutes to complete, though this can vary based on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: While primarily a road cycling route, it features a mix of surfaces. You'll encounter 56.6 km of state roads, 29.1 km of other roads, and 1.09 km of secondary roads. However, be prepared for nearly a kilometer (952 meters) of trails, as some segments of this route may be unpaved and difficult to ride. This unique characteristic adds to its challenge.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience spectacular panoramas throughout the ride. Key highlights include Views of the Zapedo marsh and the Ría de Oyambre, known for birdwatching. The route also offers a stunning Section between sea and mountains, providing views of the Picos de Europa seemingly 'kissing the sea at sunset'. You'll also pass the iconic Puente de la Maza and the scenic Collada de Carmona, with the classic postcard view of San Vicente de la Barquera.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the diverse terrain and coastal sections, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant weather for cycling. Summers can be warm, and winters might bring colder temperatures and potentially more challenging conditions on the unpaved sections. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Since this is a circular route passing through several towns, you have options for parking. Comillas or San Vicente de la Barquera are good starting points, offering various parking facilities. It's advisable to check local parking regulations and availability upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily designed for road cycling and includes significant distances on public roads and some unpaved trails. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, bringing a dog on a demanding 87.7 km road cycling route might not be ideal for their safety or comfort. If you do bring a dog, ensure they are well-trained, can keep pace, and are always on a leash, especially in populated areas and near wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need special permits or to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and trails. However, as parts of the route pass through Parque Natural de Oyambre, it's important to be aware of and respect any specific regulations for the natural park.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations for cycling within Parque Natural de Oyambre?
faqs.answer: Yes, as parts of the route traverse Parque Natural de Oyambre, it's crucial to respect its conservation rules. This includes staying on marked paths, not disturbing wildlife, and leaving no trace. While cycling is generally permitted on designated routes, be mindful of protected areas, especially around the Marisma de Zapedo, which is a significant birdwatching spot.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the Northern Route (Cantabria) and the Camino Lebaniego. You'll also find smaller segments overlapping with trails like Saja Nansa / El Pájaro Amarillo and Transcantábrica.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through charming towns like Comillas and San Vicente de la Barquera, where you'll find various cafes, pubs, and accommodation options. These towns are excellent spots to refuel, grab a meal, or even stay overnight if you plan to split the ride.
